revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 7 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 7-ngie̍t]][[ja:7月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 7월]][[szy:nu ditu'an a bulad]][[th:เดือน 7]][[za:Ndwencaet]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 7 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:七月]][[zh:农历七月]]
{{Lunisolar July|qq=}} '''Lunisolar July''' (旧暦七月) is the seventh {{ill|Month|en|Month|qid=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|qid=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}).
Under definitions preceding the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|qid=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar|1=Tenpō calendar}}, July was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Chushu|en|Chushu|qid=Q1147873|lt=Shosho|1=Chushu}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=The new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|July|en|July|qid=Q121|lt=July|1=July}} to early {{ill|September|en|September|qid=Q123|lt=September|1=September}}.
Another name for July is {{nihongo|'''Fumizuki'''|ふみづき/ふづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|July|ja|7月|qq=draft}}. An alternative name is “Mōshū (孟秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, some years have July ending on the 29th, with no July 30th.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the {{ill|July (Lunisolar)|ja|7月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar July|lt_ja=旧暦7月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Fumizuki''' (ふづき、ふみづき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for July of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125314718}}. The most widely accepted origin theory holds that Fumizuki stems from the custom on {{ill|Qixi Festival|en|Qixi Festival|qid=Q13423122|lt=Tanabata|1=Qixi Festival}} (July 7) of offering poems and airing books to the night breeze. However, because the Tanabata festival was introduced from China in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} and was not originally Japanese, another theory suggests that it refers to the month when rice ears become plump, hence names like “Fukumizuki” (含み月; ふくみづき) or “Hofukumizuki” (穂含み月; ほふくみづき). Other alternate names include “Akihadzuki” (秋初月; あきはづき) and “Nanayozuki” (七夜月; ななよづき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|qid=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}, July is called “Kunneyas Cup” (クンネヤㇱチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|qid=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: kunne yas cup), meaning “month of night‐net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
The English month name {{lang|en|July}} is derived from [[:en:Gaius Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]] ({{lang|la|Julius Caesar}}), the Roman statesman of the late {{ill|Roman Republic|en|Roman Republic|qid=Q17167|lt=Roman Republic|1=Roman Republic}} who established the {{ill|Julian calendar|en|Julian calendar|qid=Q11184|lt=Julian calendar|1=Julian calendar}}. In 45 BC, when he adopted the Julian calendar, he renamed the month from {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, meaning “fifth month,” to his family name. The English name {{lang|en|August}} is named after {{ill|Augustus|en|Augustus|qid=Q1405|lt=Augustus|1=Augustus}} (Greek {{lang|el|Αύγουστος}}); see also {{ill|August|en|August|qid=Q122|lt=August|1=August}}.
July begins on the same day of the week as April in that year, and in a {{ill|Leap year|en|Leap year|qid=Q19828|lt=leap year|1=Leap year}} also the same as January.
==Alternate names== * Ominaeshizuki・Wominaeshizuki (女郎花月) * Kenshingetsu (建申月) * Shingetsu (親月) * Tanabataduki (七夕月) * Togetsu (桐月) * Nanayozuki (七夜月) * Hatsuaki (初秋) * Fudzuki (七月・文月) * Fumihirogeduki (文披月) * Medeaiduki (愛逢月) * Rangetsu (蘭月) * Ryogetsu (涼月) Although the numerals “7” and the character {{ill|seven|ja|七|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 7|1=7|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q23350}} can be read as either “shichi” or “nana,” historically “shichi” is the older pronunciation. The use of the daiji characters {{ill|daiji '質'|ja|質|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daiji '質'|1=質|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11635108}} and {{ill|daiji '漆'|ja|漆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daiji '漆'|1=Toxicodendron vernicifluum resin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q104224503}} for {{ill|seven|ja|七|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 7|1=7|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q23350}} also stems from the reading “shichi.” Because of these conventions, the month has traditionally been called “shichigatsu,” but in recent years—particularly among young people—the conscious use of “nanagatsu” has increased to avoid confusion with “ichigatsu” (January), and even in official government announcements “nanagatsu” may be used.
== Fumizuki Dates == {| class="wikitable" |+ Fumizuki Dates ! Gregorian Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! Number of Days |- | 2014 || {{ill|July 27|en|July 27|qid=Q2719|lt=July 27|1=July 27}} || {{ill|August 24|en|August 24|qid=Q2825|lt=August 24|1=August 24}} || 29 days |- | 2015 || {{ill|August 14|en|August 14|qid=Q2775|lt=August 14|1=August 14}} || {{ill|September 12|en|September 12|qid=Q2849|lt=September 12|1=September 12}} || 30 days |- | 2016 || {{ill|August 3|en|August 3|qid=Q2786|lt=August 3|1=August 3}} || {{ill|August 31|en|August 31|qid=Q2830|lt=August 31|1=August 31}} || 29 days |- | 2017 || {{ill|August 22|en|August 22|qid=Q2828|lt=August 22|1=August 22}} || {{ill|September 19|en|September 19|qid=Q2896|lt=September 19|1=September 19}} || 29 days |- | 2018 || {{ill|August 11|en|August 11|qid=Q2778|lt=August 11|1=August 11}} || {{ill|September 9|en|September 9|qid=Q2856|lt=September 9|1=September 9}} || 30 days |- | 2019 || {{ill|August 1|en|August 1|qid=Q2788|lt=August 1|1=August 1}} || {{ill|August 29|en|August 29|qid=Q2816|lt=August 29|1=August 29}} || 29 days |- | 2020 || {{ill|August 19|en|August 19|qid=Q2829|lt=August 19|1=August 19}} || {{ill|September 16|en|September 16|qid=Q2897|lt=September 16|1=September 16}} || 29 days |- | 2021 || {{ill|August 8|en|August 8|qid=Q2780|lt=August 8|1=August 8}} || {{ill|September 6|en|September 6|qid=Q2858|lt=September 6|1=September 6}} || 30 days |- | 2022 || {{ill|July 29|en|July 29|qid=Q2717|lt=July 29|1=July 29}} || {{ill|August 26|en|August 26|qid=Q2820|lt=August 26|1=August 26}} || 29 days |- | 2023 || {{ill|August 16|en|August 16|qid=Q2812|lt=August 16|1=August 16}} || {{ill|September 14|en|September 14|qid=Q2847|lt=September 14|1=September 14}} || 30 days |- | 2024 || {{ill|August 4|en|August 4|qid=Q2784|lt=August 4|1=August 4}} || {{ill|September 2|en|September 2|qid=Q2857|lt=September 2|1=September 2}} || 30 days |- | 2025 || {{ill|August 23|en|August 23|qid=Q2821|lt=August 23|1=August 23}} || {{ill|September 21|en|September 21|qid=Q2891|lt=September 21|1=September 21}} || 30 days |}
== See Also == {{月 (暦)|Lunisolar July|List of Lunisolar Dates|月 (旧暦)|7|日 (旧暦)|[[7月30日 (旧暦)|30]]|qq=}}
